title: Lemon curd
subtitle: Smooth, tangy lemon preserve
excerpt: Eggs, butter, sugar, and lemon juice cooked together to a thick, glossy curd that is sharp, silky, and intensely lemony. Better by a distance than anything from a jar. Makes two 300 ml jars.
sourceNotes: Lemon curd is a British invention from the nineteenth century, originally used as a filling for pastry cases. The combination of eggs, butter, sugar, and citrus juice produces a set similar to a cream sauce through egg protein coagulation at low heat. Documented by Delia Smith in Complete Cookery Course (1978), Marguerite Patten, and Pam Corbin in River Cottage Handbook No. 2: Preserves (2008).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months in a sealed jar with room to expand.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps refrigerated for up to four weeks in sealed jars.

content.text: Lemon curd sets through egg protein, not pectin or gelatin. This means it is sensitive to heat: too high and the eggs scramble, giving a grainy, lumpy result. Cook it over a bain-marie (a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water) and stir constantly. The curd thickens gradually over about 10 minutes and coats the back of a spoon when it is ready.

Sterilise two 300 ml jars by placing them in an oven at 120°C for 15 minutes. Whisk together {{eggs}} eggs, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, the juice and zest of {{lemon}} lemons in a heatproof bowl. Place the bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water, making sure the base of the bowl does not touch the water. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter cubes. Stir constantly with a wooden spoon or spatula for 10–15 minutes until the curd thickens enough to coat the back of the spoon. Remove from the heat. Pour into the hot sterilised jars through a sieve if you want a smoother result. Seal immediately. Refrigerate once cool.

items.cause: Heat too high and the eggs scrambled instead of setting slowly.
items.fix: The water should barely simmer — a few bubbles, not a rolling boil. If the curd scrambles, pass immediately through a fine sieve; some of it can be rescued.

items.cause: Not enough heat, or water in the bowl touching the base and cooling the curd.
items.fix: Check the water level — it should be below the bowl base. Stir constantly: stopping allows the eggs to cook unevenly and slow the process.

items.cause: Lemons were very acidic or very large.
items.fix: Start with 2 lemons and taste before adding the third. Adjust the sugar by 20 g in either direction to balance the acidity.

content.text: Lemon curd keeps for four weeks in the fridge and is one of the most versatile preserves in the British tradition. It fills tarts and sandwiches sponge cakes, spreads on toast and scones, stirs into yoghurt, and folds into whipped cream for a lemon fool. It also works well as a filling for meringue nests, or as a base for lemon trifle. The flavour is brightest in the first two weeks.
